Items.Title: 我停止使用 Visual Studio Code | I stopped using Visual Studio Code
Items.Description:
不是因为它不好，一点也不，老实说，我仍然将它用于我的博客🤫。

但是因为我开始使用JetBrains 产品 Webstorm并且喜欢它。

让我解释一下为什么我如此喜欢这个。 它有几个改变游戏规则的元素。

全功率但完全可定制

我认为第一次，我被所有的面板、菜单和操作压得喘不过气来。

但是，您很快就会意识到它们确实有用，但在您了解这一点之前，您可以删除面板以使其成为超级干净的编辑器。

打开基本编辑器的示例。

全部在 - 开箱即用

当我下载 WebStorm 时，我寻找了令人惊叹的插件，但很快意识到你甚至不需要它们。

它已经安装了很多开箱即用的好东西，你很少需要插件。

如果你这样做了，他们的插件生态系统也是巨大的。

开箱即用的一些示例：

  • 所有 JavaScript 自动完成，甚至可以为您解决问题
  • 智能重构
  • 测试！
  • 快速文件搜索和全局搜索
  • 实时共享内置
  • 惊人的主题
  • 包括源代码控制
  • 以及更多

如果您需要插件，请查看 Jetbrains 网站上的插件仓库。

搜索作品

我们可以使用双班搜索来搜索文件和操作。
这是一种按名称、操作、插件等查找文件的强大方法。

或者，我们会获得查找文件，可用于查找文件中的任何代码！
再次超级强大的搜索功能。

测试

如果您正在编写大量测试，WebStorm 将为您提供支持！
它提供了超级健壮的测试流程，您可以在其中快速测试单个测试用例，甚至快速查看快照。

测试单个测试或文件非常容易。
您甚至可以在 WebStorm 中快速调试您的测试。

代码检查

它附带的另一个不错的选择是代码检查，这有许多令人难以置信的好处。

它可以快速检测到您的 ESLint 规则和未使用的方法可能存在的任何问题。

它还会告诉你什么时候你在做一些没有意义的事情，比如不包括图像上的 alt 标签。

它可以帮助您编写健壮的代码的代码检查列表还在继续。

速度

它毫不犹豫地运行项目，并且重构广泛使用的导入没有问题。

自从我开始使用 WebStorm 以来，它没有出现过一个问题（现在大约三个月）。

我之前遇到过 VSC 崩溃，尤其是在打开大文件或重构广泛使用的导入文件时。

结论

我不是来告诉你必须转换的。
只是分享我切换的原因以及我在 WebStorm 中看到的好处。

明显的缺点是，与免费的 VSC 相比，它是一种付费产品。

感谢您的阅读，让我们联系吧！

感谢您阅读我的博客。 随时订阅我的电子邮件通讯并在Facebook或Twitter 上联系



原文:

Not because it's bad, not at all, and to be fully honest, I use it for my blog still 🤫.

But because I started using Webstorm, a JetBrains product and love it.

Let me explain why I love this one so much. There are a couple of game-changing elements to it.

Full-power but fully customizable

I think the first time, I was overwhelmed by all the panels, menus, and actions.

However, you quickly realize they do have a use, but until you learn about that, you can remove panels to make it a super clean editor.

Example of the basic editor opening up.

All in - Out of the box

One of the great features of Visual Studio Code is that it has so many amazing plugins available to make your experience better.

When I downloaded WebStorm, I looked for amazing plugins but quickly realized you don't even need them.

It has so many great things already installed out of the box that you rarely need a plugin.

And if you do, their plugin ecosystem is also huge.

Some examples of things that work out of the box:

  • All JavaScript autocomplete and can even fix issues for you
  • Smart refactoring
  • Testing!
  • Fast file search and global search
  • Live share build-in
  • Tailwind classes autocomplete
  • Amazing themes
  • Source control included
  • and much more

If you need a plugin, check out the plugin repo on the Jetbrains website.

Search works

The cool part about WebStorm is that it has perfect search functionality.

We can search for either file and actions using the double shift search.
This is a powerful way to find files by name, actions, plugins, etc.

Alternatively, we get find-in files, which can be used to find any code in your files!
Again super powerful search functionality.

Tests

If you are writing a lot of tests, WebStorm has your back!
It provides a super robust testing flow, where you can quickly test singular test cases and even quick-view snapshots.

It's super easy to test single tests or files.
You can even quickly debug your tests in WebStorm.

Code inspection

Another great option it comes with is code inspection, and this has many incredible benefits to it.

It can quickly detect any issues you might have with your ESLint rules and unused methods.

It will also tell you when you are doing things that don't make sense, like not including alt tags on images.

And the list goes on of code inspection it can do to help you write robust code.

Speed

Another great thing about WebStorm is that it's simply super quick.
It runs projects without hesitation and has no issue refactoring a widely used import.

Since I started using WebStorm, it hasn't had a single issue (about three months now).

VSC was also pretty fast to me, but WebStorm feels a bit more stable. I had VSC crash on me before, especially while opening large files or refactoring widely used imported files.

Conclusion

I'm not here to tell you that you have to switch.
Just sharing why I switched and what benefits I see in WebStorm.

The obvious con is that it's a paid product compared to VSC, which is free.

Thank you for reading, and let's connect!

Thank you for reading my blog. Feel free to subscribe to my email newsletter and connect on Facebook or Twitter

Hi everyone!

Following the successful launch of Docusaurus 2.0, I didn't have time to send out the newsletter scheduled for earlier this week 😅

This is another special edition, on a Friday night, covering almost 3 weeks of news, and a lot has happened!

Impossible to read everything or comment 😬. I made the choice to be exhaustive and include all the links that seemed interesting to me, without necessarily commenting on them. I would already have to read them myself before 😅 a bit overwhelmed now.

During this month of August, I will focus on my Revue migration to ConvertKit. The next edition will be around August 20th, but it's also possible I skip it and won't send anything until early September.

Items.Title: 出错时的 HTML 后备图像 | HTML fallback images on error
Items.Description:
前几天我遇到了一些无法加载外部源图像的问题。

在这种情况下，我保存了指向某人 Twitter 个人资料图片的链接。 当用户更改此图像时，它不会自动反映新图像。

所以我决定研究后备图像。
这非常容易。

我们想要达到的目标：

  • 加载图像
  • 如果它没有加载，显示回退

注意：或者，您可以决定删除图像

错误后备图像上的 HTML

让我们设置一个示例 HTML 实验。

 <img src= "https://images.pexels.com/photos/163822/color-umbrella-red-yellow-163822.jpeg" />

这将加载来自 Pexels（库存图片网站）的 Pixabay上的照片。

这将完美地工作，但现在让我们尝试通过在图像中随机添加一些数字来破坏它。

 <img src= "https://images.pexels.com/photos/163822/color-umbrella-red-yellow-00000.jpeg" />

有了这个，我们得到了超级烦人的破碎图像。

那么当这种情况发生时我们能做些什么呢？
我们可以使用图像上的onerror属性来设置回退。

它是这样工作的：

 <img src= "https://images.pexels.com/photos/163822/color-umbrella-red-yellow-00000.jpeg" onerror= "this.onerror=null; this.src='https://images.pexels.com/photos/159868/lost-cat-tree-sign-fun-159868.jpeg'" />

我们使用onerror将错误设置为 null 并将图像的 src 设置为后备。
在我们的例子中，是一张失踪猫的照片。

注意：依赖外部图像不是一个好习惯。 理想情况下，您希望在文件系统中本地拥有后备图像，因此它是可靠的后备。

您可以在下面的 CodePen 中看到它的实际效果。
第一个图像加载，第二个图像损坏。

原文:

The other day I encountered some issues with images for external sources not loading.

I saved a link to someone's Twitter profile image in this case. When the user changes this image, it doesn't auto reflect the new one.

So I decided to look into fallback images.
And it's surprisingly easy.

What we want to achieve:

  • Load the image
  • If it doesn't load, show the fallback

Note: Alternatively, you could decide to remove the image

HTML on error fallback images

Let's set up a sample HTML experiment.

<img src="https://images.pexels.com/photos/163822/color-umbrella-red-yellow-163822.jpeg"
/>

This will load an Photo by Pixabay from Pexels (a stock image site).

This will work perfectly, but now let's try and destroy it by randomly adding some numbers to the image.

<img src="https://images.pexels.com/photos/163822/color-umbrella-red-yellow-00000.jpeg"
/>

With this, we get the super annoying broken image.

So what can we do when this happens?
We can use the onerror attribute on the image to set a fallback.

It works like this:

<img src="https://images.pexels.com/photos/163822/color-umbrella-red-yellow-00000.jpeg" onerror="this.onerror=null; this.src='https://images.pexels.com/photos/159868/lost-cat-tree-sign-fun-159868.jpeg'"
/>

We use the onerror to set the error to null and set the src of the image to a fallback.
In our case, a photo of a missing cat.

Note: It's not a great practice to rely on external images. You ideally want to have the fallback image locally in the file system, so it's a reliable fallback.

You can see it in action in the following CodePen.
The first image loads, and the second one is broken.

Items.Title: L8 SWE 解释软件工程师的职业生涯 | L8 SWE Explains Career for Software Engineers
Items.Description:
大家好，我叫坤。 我是 Microsoft 的合作伙伴级工程师。 在这篇文章中，我将向您解释软件工程师职业道路的全貌，以及使工程师能够向更高水平前进的关键技能。

如果您想在 YouTube 上观看与视频相同的内容，请单击此处。

调平系统

根据你加入的公司，他们对工程师的水平和期望的定义可能会有所不同，但总的来说，他们只是土豆大饼。

如果您在levels.fyi上查看大型科技公司的分级系统，您会看到结构相似性。 为简单起见，我将使用 Google 的术语。

您可以看到它从 L3 开始，这通常是大学招聘的入门级别。 然后到 L4、L5，直到 L10。 但这些水平意味着什么，除了得到越来越多的报酬？ 为了理解这一点，您首先需要了解这些公司的运作方式。

科技公司如何运作

想象一下，我们创办了一家公司，其使命是让所有宠物爱好者都能观看有趣的宠物视频。 我们的产品愿景是一款类似于 TikTok 的应用程序，只是它仅适用于宠物视频。

现在，我们需要弄清楚我们将如何实现这一愿景。 这是我们定义公司战略的地方。

经过大量的功课和讨论，我们认为要想取得成功，我们基本上需要做两件事——找到好的宠物视频，并拥有一个允许人们观看它们的应用程序。

按照这个策略，我们创建了两个团队。 一种专注于将好的宠物视频输入系统； 另一个专注于构建和改进应用程序。

被指派寻找视频的团队决定我们需要分两个阶段解决这个问题。 在前 6 个月内，构建爬虫和管道以识别和导入来自 YouTube 的优质宠物视频。 一旦应用程序拥有更多的用户，他们是宠物爱好者，他们的手机上肯定会有很多好的宠物视频，这意味着如果应用程序有上传它们的用户流，我们可以获得很多视频。

现在，我们如何支持上传？ 我们需要在应用程序中构建一个前端用户流，以及一个存储视频的后端。

前端用户流程可以进一步分解为许多更小的任务，例如在应用程序中添加一个上传按钮，构建一个简单的视频编辑 UI，以及调用后端来实际上传视频。

看那个！ 我们刚刚制定了一个非常好的和可操作的计划！ 现在，让我们仔细看看我们是如何到达这里的。

首先，我们定义了我们的公司战略，该战略为拥有两个团队的组织结构提供了依据。

然后，每个团队都有自己的重点，必须弄清楚他们的长期计划。 然后将这些计划分解为里程碑。

每个里程碑通常都需要解决一些关键问题。 每个问题都必须有一组解决方案，每个解决方案都可以分解为特定的任务。

主要期望

现在考虑到这一点，很容易理解我们之前看到的软件工程师的各个级别。

值得注意的是，大型科技公司规模如此之大，以至于它们被结构化为部门或组织的层次结构，其复杂性可能需要 L9 及以上级别。 为简单起见，我们假设我们的公司或组织不超过几百人。 他们需要确保为他们的团队制定一个良好的长期计划，该计划通常会分解为短期里程碑。

然后预计 L6 将有助于实现这些短期里程碑。 他们必须确定需要解决的关键问题，并帮助确保团队真正解决了这些问题。

然后 L5 将拥有这些关键问题中的每一个，并找出正确的解决方案。

然后，L3 需要以良好的质量实现这些任务中的每一个。

那么，促销活动如何运作？ 工程师从一个级别到下一个级别需要确定哪些关键事项？ 不同的公司对此的框架略有不同，这也取决于文化，有时还取决于组织的领导力。 这就是为什么如果你问不同的人，你可能会听到不同的答案。

根据我的经验，我建立了一个简单的心智模型，它也可能对你有所帮助。

基本上，要成功成为 L3，您需要确定的是始终如一的生产力。 您将获得分配的特定任务，并且您需要确保它们及时且高质量地完成。

但是，要达到 L4，您需要确定的是独立性。 你不能再指望别人给你分配小任务。 您将被要求构建一个功能，通常具有明确定义的规范，并且您需要在无需其他人主动介入的情况下完成该功能。

达到 L5 的关键是处理歧义的能力。 你开始拥有没有明确解决方案的问题，而你将成为解决这个问题的人。 如果问题足够复杂，您可能还需要带领一个小团队来解决它们。

让 L5 过渡到 L6 的是责任感和影响力。 您将负责实现团队的目标，并且您需要尽一切努力实现目标。 这通常需要良好的分析和优先排序技能来确定最有影响力的问题来解决，并影响 L5 和其他学科一起工作并实际解决问题。

要在 L7 及以上级别取得成功，您必须具有战略性并学会扩展。 您现在要对团队的长期成功负责，这需要更严格的规划和战略思考。

例如，以更可持续的成功为代价实现短期目标现在成为您的问题，您需要帮助团队做出正确的权衡。

特别是对于 L8 及以上，范围也变得如此庞大和复杂，以至于您需要弄清楚如何在没有直接控制细节的情况下取得成功。 例如，您需要开始关心团队的文化，以便数百人在面临模棱两可时做出正确的选择，而不会成为您参与的瓶颈。 您的影响力通常还需要超越您自己的组织，因为在您的范围内取得成功开始依赖于许多跨界合作伙伴关系。

要到L9，你需要做的就是在这里订阅我的频道，等待我的下一次晋升。 我会告诉你这一切。 好的，我们刚刚讨论了软件工程师的水平和期望。 我真的很好奇——有什么让你感到惊讶的吗？ 有的话请在下方评论。

在我即将发布的帖子中，我将详细介绍并分享我发现对各个级别的人有用的提示和技巧。 下次见！



原文:

Hello everyone, my name is Kun. I'm a partner level engineer at Microsoft. In this post, I’m going to explain to you a full picture of software engineers’ career path, and the key skills that enable engineers to advance towards higher levels.

Click here if you'd like to watch the same content as a video on YouTube instead.

The leveling system

Depending on what company you joined, there may be some differences in how they define levels and expectations for engineers, but largely speaking they are just potato po-tah-toes.

If you look at the leveling systems of big tech companies on levels.fyi, you can see the structural similarity. For the sake of simplicity, I’m going to use Google’s terminology.

You can see that it starts with L3, which is typically the entry level for college hires. And then it goes to L4, L5, up to L10. (or L11 if you’re Jeff Dean).

But what do these levels mean, outside of being paid more and more? In order to understand that, you first need to know how these companies work.

How tech companies work

Imagine we started a company whose mission is to give all the pet lovers funny pet videos to watch. Our product vision is an app similar to TikTok except that it’s only for pet videos.

(I know, it sounds lame, but for the sake of giving an example, stay with me.)

Now, we need to figure out how we would achieve that vision. This is where we define our company strategy.

After lots of homework and discussions, we figured that in order to succeed, we basically need to nail two things — find good pet videos, and have an app that allows people to watch them.

Following this strategy, we created two teams. One that focuses on getting good pet videos into the system; and another that focuses on building and improving the app.

The team that was assigned to find videos decided that we would need to tackle that in two phases. In the first 6 months, build crawlers and pipelines to identify and import good pet videos from YouTube. And once the app has more users, who are pet lovers, they must have lots of good pet videos on their phones, which means we could get lots of videos if the app has a user flow to upload them.

Now, how do we support uploads? We need to build a frontend user flow in the app, and also a backend to store the videos.

The frontend user flow can be further broken down into many smaller tasks, for example adding an upload button into the app, building a simple video editing UI, and calling the backend to actually upload the videos.

Look at that! We’ve just built a really good and actionable plan! Now, let’s take a closer look at how we got here.

First, we defined our company strategy which informed the organizational structure of having two teams.

And then, each team had their own focus and had to figure out their long term plan. These plans were then broken down into milestones.

Each milestone usually requires tackling a few key problems. Each problem has to have a set of solutions, and each solution can be broken down into specific tasks.

Key expectations

Now with that in mind, it’s really easy to understand the various levels for software engineers that we looked at earlier.

L8s and above are accountable for success at company/organization level. It’s worth noting that big tech companies are so big that they get structured into a hierarchy of divisions or organizations and their complexity might require L9s and above. For simplicity, let’s assume our company or organization is no more than a few hundred people. L8s need to figure out the strategy for such organizations and make sure it’s executed successfully by the sub teams.

L7s are typically accountable for the long term success of each team. They need to make sure there is a good long term plan for their team, which typically gets broken down into shorter term milestones.

L6s are then expected to help achieve these short term milestones. They have to identify the key problems that need to get solved, and help make sure the team actually solves them.

L5s will then own each of these key problems, and figure out the right solution.

L4s are expected to build these solutions, which requires them to break the solutions down into smaller tasks.

L3s need to then implement each of these tasks with good quality.

So, how do promotions work? What are the key things engineers need to nail to go from one level to the next? Different companies have slightly different frameworks for this, and it also depends on the culture, and sometimes the leadership of your organization. This is why you might hear different answers if you ask different people.

From my experience, I’ve built a simple mental model that might be helpful to you as well.

Basically, to succeed as an L3, what you need to nail is consistent productivity. You will get assigned specific tasks, and you need to make sure they are completed in a timely manner and with good quality.

To get to L4 however, what you need to nail is independence. You can no longer expect others to assign you small tasks. You will be asked to build a feature, typically with a well-defined spec, and you need to accomplish that without others having to proactively step in.

The key to get to L5 is the ability to deal with ambiguity. You start to own problems that don’t have clear solutions and you will be the one that figures that out. If the problems are complex enough, you might need to lead a small crew to tackle them as well.

What gets L5s to transition into L6 is accountability and influence. You will be accountable to hitting your team’s objectives, and you need to do whatever it takes to get there. This typically requires good analytical and prioritization skills to identify the most impactful problems to solve, and influence L5s and other disciplines to work together and actually solve the problems.

To be successful as L7 and above, you would have to be strategic and learn to scale. You are now accountable for your team’s success over a longer term, which requires more rigorous planning and strategic thinking.

Achieving short term objectives at the cost of more sustainable success for example now becomes your problem, and you need to help the team make the right tradeoffs.

Especially for L8 and above, the scope also gets so big and complex that you need to figure out how to succeed without having direct control over the details. For example, you need to start to care about the culture of the team so that hundreds of people can make the right choices when facing ambiguity without being bottlenecked on your involvement. Your influence would also often need to scale beyond your own organization, because achieving success at your scope starts to depend on a lot of cross-boundary partnerships.

To get to L9, what you need to do is to subscribe to my channel here, and wait for me to get my next promotion. And I’ll tell you all about it. :)

Okay, we just went over the levels and expectations for software engineers. I’m really curious — was there anything surprising to you? Please comment below if there was.

In my upcoming posts, I will go into more details and share tips and tricks that I’ve found useful for people at each level. See you next time!

Items.Description:
舍尔帕根

发表于程序的正常关闭涉及

  • 发送 SIGTERM(通知
    程序将被杀死），在收到此信号后，程序停止接收进一步的请求（可能是 Web 请求、数据库操作等）

  • 完成正在进行的/未决的任务（进行中的数据）

  • 释放资源（文件锁、内存）并终止（退出状态为 0）

流程应该能够抵御突然死亡（例如电源故障、硬件故障）。 对于这种情况，建议使用健壮的消息队列（ beanstalkd ）

概念-

  • 生产者将作业放入队列（例如 json、xml）
  • 消费者监控 Queue 并获取/保留可用作业
  • 消费者从队列中删除作业

阅读可处置性

原文:

sherpaurgen

Posted on Aug 6

Graceful shutdown of a program involves

  • Sending SIGTERM(notify
    program that its going to be killed) ,upon receiving this signal the program stops receiving further requests(could be web requests, database operations etc)

  • Finalize ongoing/pending tasks(in-flight data)

  • Release resources (file locks, memory) and terminate(with exit status 0)

Process should be robust against sudden death(eg. power failure, hardware failures). Using robust message queue(beanstalkd) is recommended for such scenarios

Concept-

  • producer puts job in the Queue(eg. json, xml)
  • consumer monitors that Queue and takes/reserve the available job
  • consumer deletes the job from Queue

Read disposability

Graceful shutdown example for golang
https://pkg.go.dev/os/signal#Notify

package main

import (
    "context"
    "fmt"
    "log"
    "net/http"
    "os"
    "os/signal"
    "syscall"
    "time"
)

func main() {
    // Hello world, the web server
    helloHandler := func(w http.ResponseWriter, req *http.Request) {
        fmt.Fprintf(w, "You requessted %s - %s", req.URL, req.Method)
    }
    api := &http.Server{
        Addr:           ":8080",
        Handler:        http.HandlerFunc(helloHandler),
        ReadTimeout:    10 * time.Second,
        WriteTimeout:   10 * time.Second,
        MaxHeaderBytes: 1 << 20,
    }

    serverErrors := make(chan error, 1)
    shutdown := make(chan os.Signal, 1)
    go func() {
        log.Printf("main api listening on %s", api.Addr)
        serverErrors <- api.ListenAndServe()
    }()

    signal.Notify(shutdown, os.Interrupt, syscall.SIGTERM)
    //above is not blocking operation although waiting on shutdown channel
    select {
    case err := <-serverErrors:
        log.Fatalf("Error while listening and starting http server: %v", err)

    case <-shutdown:
        log.Println("main: Starting shutdown")
        const timeout = 5 * time.Second
        // Context - it is used for Cancellation and propagation, the context.Background() gives empty context
        ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), timeout)
        defer cancel()
        err := api.Shutdown(ctx)
        /*Shutdown gracefully shuts down the server without interrupting any active connections. Shutdown works by first closing all open listeners, then closing all idle connections, and then waiting indefinitely for connections to return to idle and then shut down.If the provided context expires before the shutdown is complete, Shutdown returns the context's error, otherwise it returns any error returned from closing the Server's underlying Listener(s).*/
        if err != nil {
            log.Printf("main: Graceful shutdown didnot complete in %v:%v", timeout, err)
            err = api.Close()
            //Close() immediately closes all active net.Listeners and any connections in state StateNew, StateActive, or StateIdle. For a graceful shutdown, use Shutdown.
        }
        if err != nil {
            log.Fatalf("main: could not stop server gracefully Error: %v", err)
        }

    }

}

Items.Title: CSS 非选择器如何工作的完整指南 | A Complete Guide to How the CSS not Selector Works
Items.Description:
在 CSS 中，通常需要在我们想要选择一个元素的帖子上签名，但在特定情况下不需要签名——比如它是否具有特定的类。 在这些情况下，我们可以使用:not()选择器来做到这一点。 让我们看看它是如何工作的。

它的工作方式是我们创建一个选择器，然后指定它不应该是什么。 例如，假设您有以下 HTML：

 <div class= "not-red" > Not Red </div> <div class= "not-red" > Not Red </div> <div> Red </div> <div> Red </div>

我们这里有一堆div ，有些不应该是红色的。 如果我们希望页面上的所有div通常都是红色的，除了.not-red元素，我们可以使用:not(.not-red)来确保它们保持不变，嗯，不是红色：

 div :not ( .not-red ) { color : red ; }

这是另一个例子。 默认情况下，所有元素都将具有 Arial 字体 - 除了.old-fashioned元素：

 <div class= "old-fashioned" > Old Fashioned Text </div> <div> Some Text </div>

我们的 CSS，其中只有非.old-fashioned元素使用 Arial 字体，如下所示：

 div :not ( .old-fashioned ) { font-family : Arial , sans-serif ; }

您可能熟悉 CSS 中的特异性概念，其中某些选择器“覆盖”其他选择器。 例如，一个class的特异性低于id ，因此任何id CSS 属性都将覆盖同一元素上的class属性。

例如，如果您的代码中有div:not(#id) ，它仍然算作具有id ，因此特异性会增加，就好像它具有id一样。 使用:not()时记住这一点很有用

关于:not()的一件令人困惑的事情是，当您尝试使用它来停止将样式应用于元素内的事物时。 例如，假设您有以下 HTML：

 <div class= "container" > <form> <div class= "input-element" > <input type= "text" /> </div> </form> <div class= "input-element" > <input type= "text" /> </div> </div>

假设您只想将样式应用于不在form元素中的input元素。 很简单，对吧？ 你可以尝试这样的事情：

 div :not ( form ) input { border : 2px solid red ; }

只有这不起作用，原因是:not()应用于每个级别 - 我们将每个输入包装在.input-element中。 这意味着:not(form)应用于.input-element ，它确实不是表单。 所以两个input元素都会有一个红色边框。 为避免这种情况，您需要删除包装元素并使输入成为表单的直接子元素：

 <div class= "container" > <form> <input type= "text" /> </form> <input type= "text" /> </div>

这样，表单中的输入将不会从div :not(form) input继承样式。

需要注意的是，有两个版本的:not()选择器 - 一个只接受一个简单的 CSS 选择器，而:not()的更新版本接受任何 CSS 选择器。

所有浏览器都支持单一、简单的:not()选择器，就像我们目前使用的一样，包括 Internet Explorer。 然而，并不是所有的浏览器都支持复杂的选择器。 使用:not中的简单选择器，您可以执行以下所有操作：

  • :not(#id)
  • :not(.class)
  • :not(element)
  • :not([attrbute])

但是，以下内容仅适用于复杂选择器：

  • :not(#id, .class, [attribute])
  • :not(#id.class)
  • :not(element#id, #id.class)

幸运的是，对复杂选择器的支持仍然很高。 目前唯一不支持复杂选择器的浏览器是 Internet Explorer 和一些移动浏览器：



原文:

In CSS, it's often necessary to sign post that we want to select an element, but not in specific circumstances - like if it has a certain class. In these instances, we can use the :not() selector to do that. Let's look at how it works.

CSS not Selector

The CSS :not() selector is broadly supported by most browsers. The way it works, is that we create a selector, and then specify what it should not be. For example, say you have the following HTML:

<div class="not-red">Not Red</div>
<div class="not-red">Not Red</div>
<div>Red</div>
<div>Red</div>

We have a bunch of divs here, and some shouldn't be red. If we want all divs in general on our page to be red, except for .not-red elements, we can use :not(.not-red) to ensure they remain, well, not red:

div:not(.not-red) { color: red;
}

Here's another example. By default, all elements will have the Arial font - except for .old-fashioned elements:

<div class="old-fashioned">Old Fashioned Text</div>
<div>Some Text</div>

Our CSS, where only non .old-fashioned elements use the Arial font, looks like this:

div:not(.old-fashioned) { font-family: Arial, sans-serif;
}

CSS not Selectors Increase Specificity

You might be familiar with the concept of specificity in CSS, where certain selectors "override" others. For example, a class has a lower specificity than an id, so any id CSS properties will override class properties on the same element.

The :not selector also affects specificity. For example, if you had div:not(#id) in your code, it still counts as having an id, so the specificity increases as if it has an id. This is useful to remember when using :not()

CSS not Selectors and the DOM structure

One confusing thing about :not(), is when you try to use it to stop styles applying to things within elements. For example, suppose you have the following HTML:

<div class="container"> <form> <div class="input-element"> <input type="text" /> </div> </form> <div class="input-element"> <input type="text" /> </div>
</div>

Let's say you want to apply a style to only input elements which are not within form elements. Simple, right? You might try something like this:

div :not(form) input { border: 2px solid red;
}

Only this won't work, and the reason why is that :not() is applied at every level - and we are wrapping each input in .input-element. That means that :not(form) is applied to .input-element, and it is indeed, not a form. So both input elements will have a red border. To avoid that, you need to remove the wrapper element and have the input be a direct child of the form:

<div class="container"> <form> <input type="text" /> </form> <input type="text" />
</div>

That way the input in the form, will not inherit the styles from div :not(form) input.

CSS not Selector Support

It is important to note that there are two versions of the :not() selector - one which accepts only a single, simple CSS selector, and the newer update to :not() which accepts any CSS selector.

All browsers support single, simple :not() selectors, like the ones we've used so far, including Internet Explorer. However, not all browsers support complex selectors. With simple selectors in :not, you can do all of the following:

  • :not(#id)
  • :not(.class)
  • :not(element)
  • :not([attrbute])

However, things like the following are only available with complex selectors:

  • :not(#id, .class, [attribute])
  • :not(#id.class)
  • :not(element#id, #id.class)

Fortunately, support for complex selectors is still quite high. The only browser not supporting complex selectors today, is Internet Explorer and some mobile browsers:

Items.Title: 另一个移动架构比较 | Yet another mobile architectures comparison
Items.Description:
有很多关于移动应用程序的不同架构模式的文章试图让你相信这个或那个架构模式是最好的。 当然，我们都知道没有灵丹妙药……不幸的是。 然而，比较最流行的移动架构模式（ MVC、MVP、MVVM、VIPER、Riblets 和 Redux ）是一个很好的练习，并找出：

  • 我们可以从他们身上学到什么，
  • 他们如何帮助我们设计我们的应用架构。

多层架构

为了比较上述架构模式，我们需要一个基线。 我选择了多层（实际上是 5 层）架构，因为它非常流行、通用并且具有实际上非常高级抽象的层/层，因此我们可以使用它们来理解移动架构模式中层的抽象级别。

有很多关于多层架构 [1] 的信息，根据一些作者发现的系统类型为 3 层（纯后端 - 数据、业务逻辑、表示），有些是 4 层或 5 层。

每一层都是高级抽象，架构只有一个限制：层中的组件只能与相邻层的组件交互。 它允许减少应用程序各部分之间的耦合，并将依赖关系图保持在可管理状态。

因此，让我们将每个移动架构模式映射到我们的基线，看看我们能找到什么。

让我们从 MVC 开始：View 可以很容易地映射到 View 层，Controller 我们可以映射到 Presentation 层，然后 Model 应该跨越其余层，这取决于您的产品是否需要它们（在大多数情况下您做）。 所以 MVC 没有给多层架构增加任何东西，实际上只是稍微简化了一点。

接下来是MVP。 我们可以将它映射到与 MVC 相同的层中——只需将 Controller 更改为 Presenter。 此外，MVP 限制了组件之间的交互，因此 Model 只能与 Presenter 交互，而 View 也只能与 Presenter 交互。 在多层架构中，我们有同样的限制，所以 MVP 再次没有添加任何内容。

唯一的区别在于 View 和 ViewModel 之间更高级的解耦——这里 ViewModel 对 View 一无所知，从 ViewModel 到 View 的所有交互都是通过将 View 层组件中的 UI 元素绑定到 ViewModel 组件中公开的属性来发生的。 因此 MVVM 将视图层和表示层之间的绑定概念添加到多层架构中。

毒蛇

毒蛇呢？ 它看起来像一只不同的野兽。 因此 VIPER 添加了 Router 层，有助于从 Presentation 层提取路由业务逻辑。 但是，更重要的是，VIPER 引入了 VIPER 模块的概念并描述了应用程序中此类模块的通用结构，由此它开始混淆层和类名称（Interactor 通常是一个没有意义的类名称因为这个名字无法帮助我们理解Interactor内部的业务逻辑）。 将应用程序拆分为模块是一个非常有用的概念，但尚不清楚这些模块如何相互交互以及如何共享某些状态。

还原

通过引入状态、订阅、reducers、动作和中间件的概念，Redux 有助于将业务层与服务和数据层完全解耦，此外，视图 + 表示层与所有其他层。 所以 Redux 并不声称是一种通用的架构模式，它只是提供了一种组织层之间交互并保持它们彼此独立的方法。

肋骨

最后是 Riblets（或 RIB）。 这种模式实际上可以1-1映射到分层模型，Services映射到Data + Service层，Interactor到业务逻辑层，Model Stream看起来像业务层内部的State，Presenter和Router映射到Presentation层，View实际上是View层. 然而，Riblets 修复了一些 VIPER 问题，模块可以使用路由器结合到层次结构中，并使用交互器进行通信和共享一些状态。

包起来

这种分析如何帮助我们选择或理解现有的移动架构模式？ 好问题。
首先，我们可以看到并非所有的设计模式都处于同一抽象级别，而且它们的意图也不同。

所以值得注意的是，Model、View、Controller 和 Presenter 不是类，而是抽象层，您需要在多层架构的每一层中提出组件、类和它们之间的交互。 它们只是提示您如何拆分应用程序的功能。

如果您想将 UI 与 Presenter 层中的组件隔离 - 您可以尝试 MVVM 绑定方式。

所以它是一个较低级别的抽象，如果你遵循 VIPER 的思想，它的自由度和灵活性就会降低，但作为学习模块化的起点是很好的。

同样，Redux 位于更低的抽象级别，如果您按原样选择它，它将极大地限制您在层/组件交互方面。 但是 Redux 为我们提供了关于状态和业务规则管理的概念，此外它可以轻松地与此角色中的上述任何模式耦合（例如，您可以尝试将 Redux 用作 MVP 模式中的模型层）。

因此，如果您将应用程序视为模块的层次结构并且您不知道从哪里开始 - 是时候学习 Riblets 并看看它是如何在那里完成的。

参考：

[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multitier_architecture , https://www.researchgate.net/figure/The-5-tier-Architecture-model_fig1_280235378 , https://www.oreilly.com/library/view/core-j2eetm-patterns/0130648841/0130648841_ch06lev1sec1.html [1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multitier_architecture , https://www.researchgate.net/figure/The-5-tier-Architecture-model_fig1_280235378 , https://www.oreilly.com/library /view/core-j2eetm-patterns/0130648841/0130648841_ch06lev1sec1.html



原文:

There are a lot of articles about different architecture patterns for mobile applications which try to convince you that this or that architecture pattern is the best one. Sure thing, we all know that there is no a silver bullet… unfortunately. However, it’s a very good exercise to compare the most popular mobile architecture patterns (MVC, MVP, MVVM, VIPER, Riblets and Redux) and find out:

  • what we can learn from them,
  • how they can help us to design our app architecture.

Multitier architecture

In order to compare the above mentioned architecture patterns we need a baseline. I chose the multitier (actually 5 tier) architecture as it’s very popular, universal and has tiers/layers that are in fact very high level abstractions so we can use them to understand the abstraction level of the layers in mobile architecture patters.

There are a lot of information about the multitier architecture [1], depending on the type of system some authors find 3 layer (pure backend — Data, Business Logic, Presentation), some 4 or 5.

Each layer is a high level abstraction and the architecture has only one restriction: components in the layer can interact only to the components from the neighbour layers. It allows to decrease the coupling between parts of the application and keep a dependency graph in a manageable state.

So let’s map each mobile architecture pattern to our baseline and see what we can find out.

MVC

Let’s start from MVC: View can be easily mapped to the View layer, Controller we can map to Presentation layer, and Model then should span over the rest of layers and it depends on your product whether you need them or not (in most cases you do). So MVC adds nothing to the multitier architecture and actually just simplifies it a bit.

MVP

Next is MVP. We can map it in the same layers as MVC — just change Controller to Presenter. Additionally, MVP restricts interactions between components so that Model can interact only with Presenter and View can interact only with Presenter as well. In the multitier architecture we have the same restrictions, so MVP again adds nothing.

MVVM

MVVM has a pretty similar mapping. View, as in the previous examples, can be mapped to View layer, View Model in this pattern usually maps to Presentation layer and Model is the rest. The only difference is in a more advanced decoupling between View and ViewModel — here ViewModel knows nothing about View and all interactions from ViewModel to View happen through the binding of UI elements in View layer components to exposed properties in ViewModel components. So MVVM adds conception of binding between View and Presentation layers to the multitier architecture.

VIPER

What about VIPER? It looks like a different beast. View as always maps to View layer, Presenter and Router go to the Presentation layer, Interactor maps to Business logic, and, Entity spans over Service and Data layers. Thus VIPER adds Router layer that helps to extract routing business logic from Presentation layer. But, what is more important, VIPER introduces conception of VIPER modules and describes a universal structure for such a module in an app and by this it starts the confusion between layers and classes names (Interactor usually is a class name that doesn’t make sense as it this name can’t help us to understand the business logic inside the Interactor). Splitting the app to modules is a very useful concept but there is no clear understanding how those modules can interact with each other and moreover share some state.

Redux

Redux seems to be the only pattern that doesn’t cover View at all and concentrates mostly on Business logic as Reducers and State, and, Data + Service Layers as Middleware. Redux is a very good attempt to define the conception of building the “backend” part of our layered model(data, service and business+state layers). By introduction the concepts of state, subscriptions, reducers, actions and middleware Redux helps to fully decouple Business layer from Service and Data layers, and additionally, View + Presentation layers from all the other layers. So Redux doesn’t claim to be a universal architecture pattern, it just provides a way to organise interactions between the layers and keep them independent from each other.

Riblets

Lastly there is Riblets (or RIBs). This pattern can actually be 1–1 mapped to the layered model, Services maps to Data + Service layer, Interactor to Business logic layer, Model Stream looks like State inside Business layer, Presenter and Router maps to Presentation layer and View is actually View layer. Riblets looks very similar to the VIPER as it helps to split your application not only to the vertical layers but horizontally to modules as well. However Riblets fixes some VIPER problems and modules can be united to the hierarchical structure using Routers and communicate and share some state using Interactors.

Wrap up

How this analysis can help us to choose or understand existed mobile architecture patterns? Good question.
Firstly, we can see that not all the design patterns are on the same level of abstraction and moreover they have different intention.

MVC and MVP are very high level design patterns, they are on the same level of abstraction as the multilayered architecture and add nothing to it. So it worths to be aware that Model, View, Controller and Presenter are not the classes but rather abstract layers and you need to come up with the components, classes and interactions between them in each layer of the multitier architecture. They are just hints how you can split functionality of your application.

MVVM is more about interaction between ViewModel(Presenter) and View. If you want to isolate UI from components in Presenter layer — you can try the MVVM way of binding.

VIPER might help you to divide your app into modules with the strictly defined structure and interactions between components in the module. So it’s a lower level of abstraction and it has less freedom and flexibility if you follow VIPER ideology but it’s good as a starting point to learn about modularisation.

Redux is the only design pattern that takes the state and the business logic very seriously. Again Redux is located on the even lower level of abstraction and will considerably restrict you in a way of layers/components interaction if you chose it as it is. But Redux gives us an idea about the state and the business rules management and additionally it can be easily coupled with any of above patterns in this role (for instance, you may try to use Redux as a Model layer in MVP pattern).

Riblets seems to be on the lowest level of abstraction and it dictates you how to make hierarchical modules in your app, how to build them, interact between components in a module and even tries to take care of the app state. So if you think of you application as a hierarchy of modules and you have no idea where to start — it’s time to learn Riblets and see how it’s done there.

References:

[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multitier_architecture, https://www.researchgate.net/figure/The-5-tier-Architecture-model_fig1_280235378, https://www.oreilly.com/library/view/core-j2eetm-patterns/0130648841/0130648841_ch06lev1sec1.html

Items.Title: 用户 Web 身份验证变得简单 | An Introduction to FACEIO; User Web Authentication Made Easy
Items.Description:
很长一段时间以来，网络世界一直停留在 OTP 代码和用于身份验证的魔术链接之间。 例如，Web 2.0 提醒我们有时会忘记复杂的安全密码，从而促使我们重新设置。 尽管有时令人沮丧，但我们必须努力接受它。 有时我们中的一些人在登录网站时懒得输入密码。 那个宏秒让你的手指在键盘上快速寻找字母、符号和数字作为密码可能会让你大汗淋漓。 多亏了技术，网站用户身份验证比我们所经历的模拟方式更易于管理。 请继续阅读以准确了解我在说什么。

对于任何 Web 应用程序，身份验证都是一项至关重要的功能。 身份验证系统允许网络所有者跟踪其用户，这主要适用于网站所有者销售产品或服务的情况。 它有助于保留一些记录以供将来参考。

在人工智能 (AI) 和机器学习 (ML) 发展之前，电子邮件密码是最常见的身份验证方法。 没过多久，技术团队就引入了一个新想法。 他们将其称为 OAuth，这让网站所有者大吃一惊，因为出于某种原因，它简化了身份验证过程。 在这里，网站所有者信任 OAuth 提供者对用户的身份验证，为了公平起见，OAuth 提供者会在验证后将用户的详细信息提供给网站所有者。

网站用户必须在 OAuth 提供商处维护一个帐户，如果他有其他不同的网站，他也会使用同一个帐户来访问其他网站。 他们引入了无密码身份验证，网站用户可以输入他们的电子邮件或用户名，然后将链接发送到他们的邮箱。 用户之后要做的就是复制邮寄的链接，然后将其复制粘贴到浏览器上，从而自动进行身份验证和登录。

这工作了一段时间，但随着世界技术的进步，新的技术被引入到游戏中。 最近的一个是 FACEIO，它在 Web 身份验证方面的受欢迎程度势不可挡。

因此，在本文中，您将了解 FACEIO 如何精确工作、如何设置您的帐户以及为什么需要尝试这一新的身份验证功能。

什么是 FACEIO？

关于这项新开发的令人振奋的事实是，FACEIO 对其工作对象没有特殊要求。 它适用于所有现代网站上的正面和常规网络摄像头，并且由于其并发的最先进的面部识别引擎，它不会要求您提供任何指纹、深度感知或传感器。

它的功能

它与任何网站或网络应用程序集成，似乎向网站用户承诺安全的面部识别。

为什么采用 FACEIO？

出于多种原因，您应该为您的网站或 Web 应用程序采用此功能。 其中一些原因是；

  • 即时身份验证：不需要密码、安全问题、魔术链接或 OTP 代码。
  • 这里的优点是成功登录后在某些情况下不使用验证码。 他们让您在获得身份验证之前拖动图像或进行一些图像分组； 有时很烦人。 有了 FACEIO，就没有“你是机器人吗？” 问题; 单击面部验证按钮后，您的帐户验证就完成了一半。
  • 隐私考虑：我们每天向网站和 Web 应用程序提供大量个人数据。 例如，在面部识别过程中，您将需要面对数据； 来自网络摄像头和摄像头的数据用于面部身份验证以外的目的。 它们 100% 符合 CCPA 和 GDPR，因此您可以相信您提供的数据是安全的。 所有面部特征的数据都是匿名存储的。
  • 无需硬件专业化：您不需要专门的硬件即可使用 FACEIO； 只有网络摄像头和智能手机摄像头适用。 今天，很难找到没有网络摄像头的电脑或带后置或前置摄像头的智能手机。 因此，更容易采用它进行身份验证。
  • 与跨浏览器非常兼容：它与 Chromium 衍生产品、Edge、Firefox 和 Chrome 配合得很好
  • 它仅在 CSS 和 JavaScript 中实现标准技术，因此外部依赖为零。

如何设置您的 FACEIO 项目

现在我们已经了解了促使我们采用 FACEIO 的原因，让我们来看看如何在您想要处理在线业务的任何时候在您最喜欢的网站上设置您的帐户以进行面部身份验证。
首先，这是您需要做的；

将 FACEIO 添加到您的 Web 应用程序或网站。

在网站或 Web 应用程序上实现这一点是最简单的事情之一。 您只需转到 FACEIO 控制台，创建一个新应用程序，然后将资源链接到您的 Web 应用程序或网站。 完成后，为了在您的网站上轻松集成 fio.js，请按照以下说明的步骤帮助您了解应用程序的工作原理：

  • 第一步是开始创建您现在已经完成的新 FACEIO。 成功创建应用程序后，您要做的下一个重要事情是集成 fio.js。 访问 FACEIO 以获取有关如何集成fio.js库的完整演示。
  • 第 2 步是，应用程序向导会自动帮助您创建此过程。 它需要选择面部识别引擎、自定义布局小部件和云存储区域、审查可能的安全问题、公众建议等。
  • 行动迅速，希望您不会迷路，在您的网站上实现 fio.js 和面部识别 JavaScript 库。 使用完公共 ID 后，启动库。
  • 好消息，现在您的 FACEIO 已准备好运行，让我们注册，然后通过人脸识别技术验证您的用户身份，就像在此HTML 样板中演示的那样。

注册用户

完成上述过程后，您现在有资格注册 FACEIO； 幸运的是，您不会出汗，因为它提供了一个 JavaScript 库。 从最近实例化的 faceio 对象调用注册方法来注册一个新用户。 通过入职，注册方法允许您在您的网站或应用程序上以交易方式注册新用户。 在模拟系统中，它等同于标准的注册/注册功能实现。

有效载荷的接受：它只是一组任意数据，可以链接到特定的用户示例，如姓名、电子邮件地址、ID 等。 在未来的身份验证成功后，此有效负载将转发回特定用户。

  • 如果您设法为您的应用程序注册了 Webhook 处理程序，则在每次成功注册后，enroll() 将向您为 Webhook 配置的 URL 发出一个 post 请求。 当此类事件发生时，FACEIO 会使用 Webhook 通知您的系统。
  • 需要permissionTimeout，它对应于等待用户授予相机访问权限的秒数。
  • 面部 ID 是 userInfo 对象的基本成员。 它是分配给主要用户的通用唯一标识。
  • 面部 ID在您的后端用作查找键，例如，在将来的身份验证中获取链接到该特定用户的数据。 在用户拒绝访问相机的权限或可能拒绝使用条款的情况下，例如，见证了带有错误代码的承诺的拒绝。 用户将收到诸如 fioErrorCode.PERMISSION_REFUSED 或 fioErrorCode 之类的通知。 如何在 FACEIO 上对用户进行身份验证？

    要启动身份验证流程，请在您的 HTML 中添加一个 ID 为“身份验证”的按钮。 在 getElementabyID 方法的帮助下，访问 index.js 中的按钮。

    然后在用户按下该按钮的那一刻启动身份验证流程。 完成后，选择permissionTimeout、idleTimeout、replyTimeout 和locale 参数，就像之前的enroll() 函数一样。

    当用户按下认证按钮时，会出现一个类似于注册功能的屏幕。 它扫描面部并要求访问相机。 扫描后，系统会要求您输入您在注册期间输入的 PIN。

    如果您提供正确的 PIN，FACEIO 会返回在注册过程中指定的 Facedata 和有效负载。

    很快，我们的身份验证流程就完成了。 与传统的电子邮件密码身份验证方法相比，您可以得出结论，FACEIO 身份验证更快更容易。 对于任何网站用户来说，数据隐私都是至关重要的。 任何网站的首次或所有用户都会担心数据安全。 如果您不允许，则无法使用 FACEIO 进行面部扫描。 此外，在任何情况下，如果任何注册用户想要选择退出面部识别技术，他们都可以这样做，甚至可以从服务器中删除面部数据。

    它于 2018 年采用该系统来保护用户的隐私和个人数据。

    遵循严格的安全准则，FACEIO 仅存储用户面部特征的哈希值。 它存储纯数据和尽可能少的信息。 此外，小部件或用户库不处理任何生物特征数据。 一切都是通过后端流程完成的。

    此外，为避免冒充，FACEIO 拒绝任何被遮盖的面孔。

    经常问的问题

    当有人试图冒充我时会发生什么？

    首先，您不必担心，因为冒充者无论如何都无法访问您的帐户。 他们将被拒绝访问，并且您将收到立即检测到的可疑活动的通知。 只要你的脸不被扫描，就没有人会登录你的账户。 相信它的安全。

    如果我面部受伤，FACEIO 会拒绝我的访问吗？

    事故无时无刻不在发生，有些会留下永久性的严重伤疤。 如果你受伤导致面部特征发生变化，FACEIO 可能会因为可能的变化而无法识别你，但你不必为此感到恐慌。 您可以联系支持团队并将更改通知他们以授予您访问帐户的权限。

    是的，它确实。 如果您使用的是私人浏览器，请启用 JavaScript。 结论

    世界对伴随这一新功能的工作动态感到惊讶。 它似乎也引起了企业和企业的大量关注。 不要犹豫，跳上火车来一次科技之旅。 传统的日子已经一去不复返了。 现在将您的安全检查从传统方式升级到数字方式。 立即采取行动，使用 FACEIO 设置您的网站用户帐户。



    • 原文:

    For a long time, the web world has been stuck between OTP codes and magic links for authentication purposes. Web 2.0, for example, reminds us of complex security passwords that sometimes we'd forget, prompting a reset. However frustrating it was at times, we had to work on accepting it. Sometimes some of us are too lazy to key in our passwords when logging into a website. That macro second's rush of running your fingers through your keyboard looking for letters, symbols, and numbers as a password can break a sweat. Thanks to technology, website user authentication have been made far more manageable than the analog ways we've been subjected to. Read on to find out exactly what I'm talking about.

    A Brief History Class about Web Authentication

    For any web application, authentication is a crucial feature. An authentication system allows the web owner to track its users, and this applies primarily where the website owner is either selling a product or a service. It helps to keep some records for reference in the future.

    Before the advancement of Artificial Intelligence(AI) and Machine Learning(ML), email passwords were the most common authentication method. It didn't take long before the tech team introduced a new idea. They called it OAuth, and it blew the minds of website owners because, for some reason, it simplified the authentication process. Here, the website owner trusted the authentication of a user to the OAuth provider, and to make it fair, the OAuth provider would give out the user's details after verification to the website owner.

    A website user had to maintain a single account with an OAuth provider, and in case he had other different websites, he’d also use the same account to access the other websites. OAuth gained the trust of its users, but another fresh idea came into play after a while.

    They introduced passwordless authentication, where a website user would key in their email or username and send a link to their mailbox. All a user had to do afterward was to copy the mailed link, then copy-paste it on the browser hence automatic authentication and login.

    That worked for some time, but as the world advances technologically, new techniques are introduced into the game. The recent one is FACEIO which is unstoppable with its popularity gain regarding web authentication.

    In this article, therefore, you'll learn how FACEIO works precisely, how you can set up your account, and why you need to try this new authentication feature.

    What is FACEIO?

    FACEIO is a digital facial recognition system or a facial authentication framework developed to be implemented on any given website to easily authenticate its users through facial recognition via a simple JavaScript snippet instead of the usual sending of OTP codes or logging in via password.

    The exhilarating fact about this new development is that FACEIO has no special requirements for what it works with. It works with frontal and regular webcams on all modern websites, and thanks to its concurrent state-of-the-art facial recognition engines, it won’t ask you for any fingerprints, depth perception, or sensors.

    It’s Function

    FACEIO provides a safe and fast web authentication technique through facial recognition from webcams and back or front smartphone cameras. It integrates with any website or web application, seemingly, promising secure facial recognition to the users of a website.

    Why Adopt FACEIO?

    You should adopt this feature for your website or web application for several reasons. Some of these reasons are;

    • Instant authentication: needs no passwords, security questions, magic links, or OTP codes.
    • The advantage here is the absence of a captcha used in some instances after a successful login. They get you to drag an image across or do some image grouping before you get authenticated; it's sometimes irritating. With FACEIO, there are no "are you a robot?" questions; once you click the facial authentication button, you are halfway done authenticating your account.
    • Privacy Considerate: we give so much personal data to websites and web applications daily. During facial recognition, for example, you will need to face data; FACEIO prioritizes data privacy as the process of authentication is strictly end-to-end encrypted. Data from webcams and cameras are used for purposes other than facial authentication. They are 100% CCPA and GDPR compliant, so you can trust that the data you provide is safe. Data of all facial features are stored anonymously.
    • No hardware specialization required: You don't need specialized hardware to use FACEIO; only the webcams and smartphone cameras are applicable. Today, it’s hard to find a computer without a webcam or a smartphone with a back or front camera. It's, therefore, easier to adopt it for authentication.
    • Very compatible with cross-browsers: its works just fine with Chromium derivatives, Edge, Firefox, and Chrome
    • It only implements standard technology in CSS and JavaScript hence zero external dependencies.

    How to Set Up Your FACEIO Project

    Now that we’ve learned what reasons are there to gear us into adopting FACEIO let's tap into how you can set up your account on your favorite website for facial authentication anytime you want to handle your online businesses.
    First things first, here's what you need to do;

    Add FACEIO to Your Web Application or Website.

    Implementing this on websites or web applications is one of the easiest things. All you need is to go to FACEIO Console, create a new application, and then link the resource to your web application or website. When done, for easy integration of fio.js on your website, follow the below explained steps to help you understand how the application works:

    • The first step is getting started on creating a new FACEIO which you have done by now. After successfully creating the application, the next important thing you'd want to do is integrate fio.js. Visit FACEIO for a full demonstration of how to integrate the fio.js library.
    • Step 2 is, automatically, the application wizard should help you create this process. It entails the selection of a facial recognition engine, customizing the layout widget and cloud storage region, reviewing the possible security questions, public recommendations, et cetera.
    • Moving swiftly, hoping you are not lost, implement the fio.js and the facial recognition JavaScript library on your website. Once you’re done using your public ID, initiate the library.
    • Good news, now that your FACEIO is ready to function, let's enroll and then authenticate you as a user via the face recognition technique just like it's demonstrated on this HTML boilerplate.

    Enrolling a User

    After completing the above process, you now qualify to be enrolled on the FACEIO; lucky, you won’t sweat a thing because it provides a JavaScript library. Call the enroll method from the recently instantiated faceio object to enroll a new user. Through onboarding, the enroll method lets you enroll a new user on your website or application transactionally. In an analog system, it is equated to a standard sign-up/register function implementation.

    The FACEIO Widget will be triggered by a compelling call to enroll () while asking for a user’s consent (if not yet authorized), request access (if not yet granted) to the browser frontal camera or webcam, and index the facial features of the user enrolled for future authentication purposes.

    Enroll () takes some Optional parameters, which include;

    • Acceptance of a payload: it’s simply an arbitrary set of data one can link to a particular user example, Name, Email Address, ID, et cetera. This payload gets forwarded back to the particular user upon successful future authentication.
    • If you managed to register a Webhook handler for your application, enroll () will make a post request to the URL you configured for the Webhook after each successful enrollment. Webhook allows you to get notified in time and receive essential data during the interaction of the FACEIO Widget with your users for synchronization and backed-up-to-date data. When such events happen, Webhooks are used by FACEIO to inform your system.
    • Enroll () also takes information on locale, which is basically for interaction language.
    • It takes permissionTimeout which corresponds to the number of seconds to wait for the user to grant permission for the camera to be accessed.
    • Enroll () returns a Promise whose fulfillment handler receives a userInfo object when the user has been successfully enrolled. Facial ID is the essential member of the userInfo object. It is a Universally Unique identification assigned to the primary user.
    • The Facial ID serves as a lookup key on your backend, for instance, to fetch data linked to this particular user on future authentication. Under the circumstance that the user denies permission to access the camera or maybe rejects the terms of use, for instance, a rejection of the promise with an error code is witnessed. The user will receive a notification such as fioErrorCode.PERMISSION_REFUSED or fioErrorCode. TERMS_NOT_ACCEPTED respectively.

    How to Authenticate a User on FACEIO?

    FACEIO authentication is straightforward. To initiate the authentication flow, in your HTML, add a button with the id "authenticate." With the help of the getElementabyID method, access the button inside index.js.

    The authentication flow is then initiated the moment the user presses that button. After you’re done, choose on permissionTimeout, idleTimeout, replyTimeout, and locale parameter like the previous enroll () function.

    When the user presses the authentication button, a screen appears resembling the one in enroll function. It scans the face and asks for camera access. Once scanned, the PIN you entered during the enrollment will be asked for.

    FACEIO returns the Facedata and the payload specified in the enrollment process if you provide the correct PIN.

    In no time, our authentication flow is completed. Compared to traditional methods of email password authentication, you can conclude that FACEIO authentication is way quicker and easier. FACEIO server and its AI model do all the crucial work, leaving your only developer task to add the application logic to modify the authentication experience and flow.

    Privacy and Security Features on FACEIO

    For any website user, data privacy is crucial. Concern about data safety arises from any website's first-time or all-time user. FACEIO adheres to all the privacy guidelines and requests for a user's consent before accessing their camera. A face scan cannot be possible with FACEIO if you don't permit it. Additionally, in any case, if any registered user feels like opting out of the face recognition technique, they are allowed to do so and even delete the facial data from the server.

    FACEIO's privacy system is completely GDPR and CCPA compliant. GDPR translates to General Data Protection Regulations. It adopted this system in 2018 to protect the user's privacy and personal data.

    CCPA on the California Consumer Protection Act handstands gives the user more control over their data.

    Following stringent security guidelines, FACEIO only stores hashes of a user’s facial features. It stores plain data and as little information as possible. Additionally, the widgets or user libraries do not handle any biometric data. Everything is done through the backend process.

    Also, to avoid impersonation, FACEIO rejects any covered faces.

    Frequently Asked Questions

    What happens when someone tries to impersonate me?'

    First, you need not worry, as the impersonator won't have access to your account by any means. They will be declined access, and you’ll be notified of the suspicious activity immediately detected. As long as your face is not scanned, no one will log into your account. Trust in its safety.

    If I get a facial injury, will FACEIO decline my access?'

    Accidents happen all the time, and some come with permanent severe scars. If you get an injury that changes your facial features, FACEIO might fail to recognize you due to the possible changes, but you don't need to panic about this. You can contact the support team and inform them of the changes to grant you access to your account.

    Does FACEIO use a Private Browser?

    Yes, it does. If you’re using a private browser, enable the JavaScript. FACEIO uses JavaScript to connect with the server.

    Conclusion

    The world is amazed by the dynamics of work accompanied by this new feature. It seems to have also snatched a lot of attention from businesses and corporates. Don't hesitate to hop on the train for a technological ride. Gone are the traditional days. FACE is here to change the game, be the key player. Upgrade your security check from the traditional ways to the digital way now. Take action today and set up your website user account with FACEIO.

  • 用服务器的 ipv4 地址添加一条A 记录。

  • 您可以添加多个具有相同 ipv4 地址的不同子域的A 记录。

仅在您的域提供商 DNS 中添加记录。 不要在服务器提供商中创建新域。

  • 通过 ssh 登录。

  • 运行以下命令

sudo apt update sudo apt upgrade -y

  • 重启您的实例。

  • 通过 ssh 再次登录。

  • 安装 Node.js

 curl -sL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_12.x | sudo -E bash - sudo apt install nodejs node --version

  • 创建服务器的文件夹并为其编写代码。

  • 示例服务器代码

const express = require('express') const app = express() const port = 3000 app.get('/', (req, res) => { res.send('Hello World!') }) app.listen(port, () => { console.log(`Example app listening at http://localhost:${port}`) })
 sudo npm i pm2 -g pm2 start <server-file-name> --name "<process name>"
 sudo ufw enable sudo ufw status sudo ufw allow ssh (Port 22) sudo ufw allow http (Port 80) sudo ufw allow https (Port 443)
 sudo apt install nginx sudo nano /etc/nginx/sites-available/default

示例服务器块看起来像

server { server_name <domain-name>; location / { proxy_pass http://localhost:<port of server>; proxy_http_version 1.1; proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade; proxy_set_header Connection 'upgrade'; proxy_set_header Host $host; proxy_cache_bypass $http_upgrade; } listen 80; }
  • 添加此项以显示所有其他 URL 的默认页面
server { server_name <IPV4 address of the server>; root /var/www/html; index index.nginx-debian.html; listen 80 default_server; }

  • Create a Ubuntu server with any provider like AWS, Linode, DigitalOcean, etc.

  • Add a ssh key.

  • Get a domain name from any provider like BigRock, Google, etc.

  • Add an A record with the ipv4 address of the server.

  • You can add multiple A records with different subdomains with the same ipv4 address.

Add the records in your domain provider DNS only. Don't create new domain in the server provider.

  • Login via ssh.

  • Run the following commands

sudo apt update

  • Reboot your instance.

  • Login again via ssh.

  • Install Node.js

curl -sL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_12.x | sudo -E bash -

sudo apt install nodejs

node --version

  • Create the folder(s) of your server and write the code for it.

  • Sample server code

const express = require('express')
const app = express()
const port = 3000

app.get('/', (req, res) => {
  res.send('Hello World!')
})

app.listen(port, () => {
  console.log(`Example app listening at http://localhost:${port}`)
})

sudo npm i pm2 -g

pm2 start <server-file-name> --name "<process name>"

sudo ufw enable

sudo ufw status

sudo ufw allow ssh (Port 22)

sudo ufw allow http (Port 80)

sudo ufw allow https (Port 443)

sudo apt install nginx

sudo nano /etc/nginx/sites-available/default

Sample server block looks like

server {
    server_name <domain-name>;

    location / {
        proxy_pass http://localhost:<port of server>;
        proxy_http_version 1.1;
        proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade;
        proxy_set_header Connection 'upgrade';
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
        proxy_cache_bypass $http_upgrade;
    }
    listen 80;
}
  • Add this to show default page for all the other URLs 
server {
    server_name <IPV4 address of the server>;
    root /var/www/html;
    index  index.nginx-debian.html;
    listen 80 default_server; 
}

sudo service nginx restart

Items.Title: 如果您像对待金融债务一样对待技术债务……您将如何衡量破产的风险？ | If You Treated Technical Debt Like You Do Financial Debt... How Would You Measure Your Risk of Going Under?
Items.Description:
今天我们深入探讨技术债务：它是什么，什么时候好，什么时候坏，如何衡量它，如何计算成本，如何解决它。

那么什么是技术债务？

很多时候，技术债务被定义为有意识地以质量换取速度，或者是糟糕的设计决策的结果。 但是，虽然这两个都是技术债务的好例子，但技术债务也可以成为绝对高质量的代码：如果它不能与技术生态系统一起发展。 但并非所有技术债务都是平等的。

下面是一个模型，您可能会发现它在映射应用程序的不同部分时很有用。

技术债务会计模型

技术债务（如金融债务）可能很有用，可以帮助您进入市场并通过 MVP 获得吸引力，或者允许您进行创新或重建。 那是当你的债务是“低利息”时，你明白权衡和痛苦就在你的未来，并计划在它变得痛苦之前偿还。

高息债务，这是有风险的，而且更难偿还。 在这里，您的技术痛苦不是在前方，而是现在，如果您不小心，它将加剧债务违约。 现在，技术债务给整个公司带来了严重的运营和商业限制，并造成了从安全漏洞到服务中断的声誉风险。

这是您的公司因技术债务而面临生存风险的地方。 您的核心平台可能已经通过了广泛宣传的关键漏洞的生命终结，并且您在您的网站标题中宣传了这一点。 你的系统变得如此臃肿且高度耦合，以至于没有人真正知道每件事是做什么的，而且每个人都害怕改变事物，因为每一次改变都会产生副作用。 您的新功能停滞不前，您的开发人员离开，您的公司停滞不前，将用户流失给更可靠的竞争对手。

技术债务会计

有了这个模型来理解和分类你的技术债务，实施的问题仍然存在：

  • 您如何严格衡量它以了解代码库的任何给定部分属于哪个类别？

  • 您如何知道哪些特定类最紧密耦合或更容易产生副作用或最难理解？

  • 您如何衡量影响，而不是技术债务对您的团队或公司的功能开发过程的影响？

  • 您如何估算其财务成本？

  • 您如何选择首先重构代码的哪些区域？

  • 你怎么知道更换是唯一的选择？

  • 您如何说服企业需要投资技术债务？

我在培训课程中介绍的衡量技术债务的指标包括代码复杂性、认知复杂性、类和方法复杂性、传入和传出耦合的公式（一个可能在代码的许多部分产生副作用，另一个可能容易受到代码许多部分的更改的影响）； 包依赖性指标等。 还有强大的可视化功能可以帮助您找到应用程序中的接缝，识别模块化或服务解耦的低悬成果，以及最高风险的区域。 上述指标也被证明可以 70-80% 预测错误，因此它们允许您识别可能容易出现错误的类和方法。

一种不同类型的度量标准是逃逸速度，即新工作将错误释放到生产中的次数。 有像szz这样的算法可以在存储库提交历史中估计这一点，包括全局和文件。

另一种方法使用 Jira 等问题跟踪器。 您可以创建查询甚至仪表板，以实时量化和监控之前标记为已完成的工单重新打开的速度。 您还可以跟踪标记为“错误”或“修复”的工单的数量和比率、响应错误和解决错误所需的时间。 这些基于流程的指标特别重要，因为它们可以帮助您量化技术债务与运营和战略债务的复合并计算成本。 您可以跟踪在错误和修复上花费了多少时间，这是从新功能中扣除的时间，以及相关的机会和竞争成本，以及可能影响采用和保留的用户痛点。

这些流程指标允许您对技术债务进行财务估算。 您可以跟踪您的产品团队（包括开发人员、经理、产品负责人、质量保证、客户服务）在错误上花费的时间，并获得按比例汇总的员工成本的相当精确的数字。 如果将其与收入、利润和营业额流进行比较，您会发现超出原始金额的成本有多么重要，因为它可能会抵消产品垂直的全部利润。 您还可以更粗略地估计路线图延迟或放缓的成本和频率，对照以它们作为机会成本代理的商业预测。 最后，您可以在客户获取和保留、错误引入率以及客户投诉之间建立关联。 这种分析对于获得企业投资以投资技术债务以及确定您的产品在我提议的框架中的位置至关重要。

技术债务还包括安全债务，因为它几乎总是包含可以通过声纳等静态分析工具或 API 测试工具跟踪和测量的漏洞。 最后，技术债务复合成环境债务，因为您可以将速度、请求、数据传输、服务器强度、基础设施配置等与更高的二氧化碳排放量、电池和 CPU 使用率以及一些下游和上游成本联系起来。 这可能会对您的 ESG 会计、您的品牌价值以及您向净零的过渡产生影响。

您可以在我最近就该主题进行的一次演讲中找到上述框架、指标和工具的说明。 事实上，您可能已经注意到我添加了一个新的技术讲座部分。 好吧，你没注意到。 它有来自各种软件工程主题的聚会、会议甚至 COP26 的录音。 我会定期在这里介绍一个。 我从 Technical Debt 开始，因为我真的很高兴能够在 11 月被国际软件架构师培训和认证标准（可能是世界上最重要的软件架构认证机构）选中就该主题进行为期 1 天的在线研讨会。

在这样一位真正的标准制定者的邀请下，在世界领先的建筑会议之一上投入 9 个小时（还有一个关于现代化巨石的后续演讲），同时感到谦卑、令人兴奋和具有挑战性。 上面的演讲可以作为我在线研讨会的试吃者，它实际上将训练你在实践中应用这个框架、工具和指标。

如果您喜欢它，您可能想报名参加（在线）研讨会。 请分享对此博客文章或上述谈话的任何反馈，以及我应该在课程前澄清或添加的内容的建议。

还可以查看整个会议和培训日，节目中有一些非常精彩的演讲！



原文:

Today we take a deep dive into technical debt: what is it, when is it good, when is it terrible, how to measure it, how to cost it, how to address it.

So what is tech debt?

Very often, tech debt is defined as a conscious trade off of quality for speed, or a result of poor design decisions. But while both of those are good examples of tech debt, tech debt can also come to absolutely top quality code: if it fails to evolve with the tech ecosystem. The cutting edge app of 2010 is very often the creaky, slow and plainful monolith of in 2020. It's not that the original choices were suboptimal: it's that time moves fast in tech, and code needs to constantly evolve to keep in sync.

But not all technical debt is created equal.

Below is a model you may find useful in mapping different parts of your application.

Technical Debt Accounting Model

### Low Interest Technical Debt

Technical debt, like financial debt, can be useful, helping you enter the market and gain traction with an MVP, or allowing you to innovate, or rebuild. That's when your debt is "low interest", you understand the trade offs and the pain is in your future, with a plan to pay it back before it gets painful.

### High Interest Technical Debt

High interest debt, that's risky, and much harder to pay off. Here your technical pain is not ahead but right now, and if you're nor careful, it will compound into a debt default. Technical debt now places serious operational and commercial constraints on the entire company, and creates reputational risks, from security breaches to service interruption.

### Technical Debt Default

This is where your company is at existential risk because of technical debt. Your core platforms may have passed End Of Life with widely publicised critical exploits, and you advertise this in your website headers. Your system has become so bloated and is so highly coupled that no one really knows what everything does, and everyone is afraid to change things because every change creates side-effects. Your new features grind to a halt, your devs leave, your company stagnates and loses users to more reliable competitors.

Technical Debt Accounting

https://youtu.be/McO_ONKxwNU

So equipped with this model for understanding and categorising your technical debt, the questions of implementation remain:

  • How do you rigorously measure it to know in which category any given part of your codebase falls?

  • How do you know which specific classes are most tightly coupled or more likely to create side effects or most difficult to understand?

  • How do you measure the impact, as opposed to the presence of technical debt on your team or company's feature development process?

  • How do you estimate its financial cost?

  • How do you choose which areas of the code to refactor first?

  • How do you know when replacement is the only option?

  • How do you persuade the business of the need to invest in technical debt?

Metrics for measuring technical debt which I cover in my training course include formulas for code complexity, cognitive complexity, class and method complexity, afferent and efferent coupling (one is likely to have side effects in many parts of the code, the other is likely to be vulnerable to changes in many parts of the code); package dependency metrics and more. There are also powerful visualisations which can help you find the seams in your application, identifying the low hanging fruit for modularising or service decoupling, as well as the highest risk areas. The metrics above have also proven to be 70-80% predictive of bugs, so they allow you to identify likely bug prone classes and methods.

A different type of metric is escape velocity, the amount of times new work releases bugs into production. There are algorithms like szz that estimate this in repository commit histories, both globally and by file.

A different approach uses issue trackers like Jira. You can create queries and even dashboards that quantify and monitor live the rate at which tickets previously marked as done are reopened. You can also track the number and rate of tickets marked as "bug" or "fix", the time it takes to respond to a bug, and to resolve it. These process based metrics are particularly important because they help you quantify and cost the compounding of technical debt into operational and strategic debt. You can track how much time is spent on bugs and fixes, and this is time deducted from new features, with associated opportunity and competitiveness costs, and also user pain points, which can affect adoption and retention.

These process metrics allow you to put financial estimates to your technical debt. You can track how many hours your product team (inc. devs, managers, product owners, qa, customer service) spend on bugs, and get pretty precise figures of pro-rata aggregated staff costs. If you compare this to revenue, profit and turnover streams, you can see how significant a cost that is beyond the raw amount, as it might negate the entire profit from a product vertical. You can also, more roughly, estimate the cost and frequency of roadmap delays or slowdowns, against commercial projections predicated on them as a proxy for opportunity costs. Finally you can make correlations between customer acquisition and retention and bug introduction rates as well as customer complaints. This kind of analysis is essential to get business buy-in for investing in technical debt, and for identifying where in my proposed framework your product might be.

Technical debt also compounds in to security debt, as it almost always entails vulnerabilities that can be tracked and measured via static analysis tools like Sonar, or API testing tools. Finally, technical debt compounds into environmental debt, as you can link speed, requests, data transmission, server intensity, infrastructure configuration and more to higher CO2 emissions, battery and CPU usage, and several downstream and upstream costs. This could have implications to your ESG accounting, your brand values, and your transition to Net Zero.

You can find an illustration of the above framework, metrics and tooling in a talk I recently gave on the subject. Indeed, you may have noticed I added a new Tech Talks section. OK, no way you noticed. It has recordings from meetups, conferences and even COP26 on a wide range of software engineering subjects. I will periodically introduce one here. I started with Technical Debt, because I am genuinely thrilled to have been selected to give a 1 day online workshop on the subject in November by the International Standard in Training and Certification of Software Architects - probably the world's foremost software architecture certification body.

To be dedicated 9 hours (there's also a follow-on talk on Modernising Monoliths) at one of the world's leading architecture conferences, at the invitation of such a literal standard setter, feels humbling, exciting and challenging, all at the same time. The talk above can be taken as a taster for my online workshop, which will actually train you to apply this framework, tools and metrics in practice.

If you enjoy it, you might want to sign up for the (online) workshop. Do please share any feedback on this blog post or the talk above, and suggestions for things I should clarify or add ahead of the course.

Also check out the entire conference and training days, there are some really fantastic talks in the programme!

Items.Title: AsyncApi 能否影响气候变化？ | Could AsyncApi Make A Dent on Climate Change?
Items.Description:
乍一看，将一个新兴的 API 规范与我们这个时代最大的生存挑战联系起来就像是在询问一种新的餐厅菜单格式是否会影响全球肥胖流行病。

然而，仔细研究表明，AsyncApi 规范通过加速和优化事件驱动的 API，可以帮助显着减少全球排放，从而增加我们进行有效路线修正的机会。 让我们分解一下。

超过自互联网推出以来过去 32 年传输的所有数据。

使用最保守的估计，该数据量相当于 7200 万兆瓦时，而大约相当于 2000 万吨二氧化碳。 这相当于驾驶 400 万辆汽车一年，并且只计算传输消耗的电力，而不是数据存储（数据中心占全球所有电力消耗的 1% ）、客户端设备电力或数据中心的隐含碳和设备。 我们需要种植620-9.2 亿棵树来抵消这么多的排放。 这些可能的保守估计受到许多细微差别、警告和技术辩论的影响，但结论是明确的：互联网数据流量是主要的污染源。

但是我们仍然没有连接到 AsyncApis！ 估计的下一步是粗略计算上述流量中有多少是由 API 专门调节的。 一个好的代理是 Akamai，它路由 15-30% 的所有互联网流量。 他们发现通过其网络的所有流量中有 83% 是 API 流量，只有 27% 是 html 网站流量。 如果这个数字换算得更广泛，这可能意味着 API 每年会产生约 1600 万吨二氧化碳。

但是什么样的 API？ 仔细观察表明，在他们的调查中，93-95% 的 API 本质上是同步命令响应 API，只有 5-7% 是异步的、事件驱动的 API。 同样，确切的数字会有所不同，但总体分布是明确的。 这些 API 中有很大一部分是通过轮询查询或查询的，其中重复调用以查看是否存在状态更改。 根据 Zapier 2013 年的估计，这些请求中有 98.5% 是空的，这意味着整个请求生命周期中涉及的所有排放都完全浪费了。 在过去的 9 年里，这种情况几乎可以肯定得到了显着改善，但这是所涉及的低效率类型的一个例子。

这让我们更接近 AsyncApi！

越来越多地从命令驱动转向事件驱动架构，在这种架构中，与其等待特定命令触发行为，不如将该行为与条件（即事件）的变化异步关联并自动化。 这具有巨大的潜力和可能性，这就是为什么它作为一种架构范式发展如此迅速的原因。 事件通知将构成超过 60% 的新数字业务解决方案的一部分。

  • 超过 50% 的商业组织将参与事件驱动的数字商业生态系统。
  • 大多数领先的应用平台供应商都将包括用于事件驱动设计的高生产力工具。

    • 从传统 REST API 和类似的命令请求方法切换的环境潜力在于，您可以将行为和消费与气候感知触发器联系起来。 当电网未在可再生能源上运行时，您可以应用优雅降级，并在可再生能源上运行时应用完整性能。 您可以让 IoT 设备在不需要持续激活时自动切换到低能耗模式。 与其依靠人们记住关闭灯或设备，不如在检测到用户的存在或不在时打开和关闭它们。 事实上，这样的架构支撑着大多数智能设备。

    采用这种范式的最大障碍之一是历史上缺乏促进互操作性的通用语言，这种范式可能会显着减少传输的数据量和互联网消耗的能源。 这就是 AsyncAPI（终于！）的用武之地了。它提供了一种标准和一种语言来描述基于事件的异步通信，不仅极大地实现了 api 间通信，而且通过使广泛的应用程序成为可能，大大缩短了开发人员的设计和实现时间。自动化范围，类似于更熟悉的 OpenApi。 这种通用语言充当通用翻译器，它允许以一种紧急的、归纳的方式相互协调的设备、工作流和工具网络的出现。

    此外，在规范发展的早期阶段，AsyncApi 社区拥有历史性的机会，可以认真关注它不能描述的任何方式，而是推动即将到来的事件驱动通信的性质，或者像 Gibson说是这里，只是分布不均。 可能有一些元素可以添加到规范中，这些元素可以极大地增加根据规范构建的所有应用程序的环境潜力。 例如，能够标记网络是否具有延迟容忍度，可能会对允许广泛的能源感知应用、扩大数字访问和包容性以及促进创新产生巨大影响，特别是在全球南方。

    这是一个非常早期的时刻，但也是一个关键时刻。 事件驱动的异步通信与云和无服务器一起是下一波（当前？）技术范式的一部分。 如果 AsyncApi 能够实现并影响从命令请求、同步和气候盲区生态系统向事件驱动、异步和环境意识范式的转变，那么，毕竟，是的，我确实相信它可以成为对世界排放量产生显着影响，从而对气候变化产生影响。



    原文:

    It may at first sight seem like linking an emergent API specification to the biggest existential challenge of our time would be like asking whether a new format for restaurant menus might impact on the global obesity epidemic.

    A closer look however suggests the AsyncApi specification, by accelerating and optimising event driven APIs, could help significantly reduce global emissions, in turn increasing our chances of impactful course correction. Let's break it down.

    CISCO estimates that by 2022 a gargantuan 4.8 zettabytes of data will be transferred over IP addresses. More than all data transferred in the previous 32 years since the Internet was launched.

    Using the most conservative estimates, that amount of data equates to 72 million megawatt hours which in turn roughly equals 20 million tonnes of CO2. This is the equivalent of driving 4 million cars for a year, and only accounts for electricity consumed in transfer, not data storage (data centres account for 1% of all electricity consumption worldwide), client device electricity, or the embodied carbon of data centres and devices. We would need to plant 620-920 million trees to offset that many emissions. These likely conservative estimates are subject to a lot of nuances, caveats and technical debate, but the conclusion is unequivocal: internet data traffic is a major source of pollution.

    But we still haven't made the connection to AsyncApis! The next step in estimations is to roughly calculate how much of the traffic above is mediated specifically by APIs. One good proxy is Akamai, which routes 15-30% of all internet traffic. They found that 83% of all traffic through their network was API traffic, and only 27% was html website traffic. If this figure translates more broadly, which is likely, that would mean that some 16 million tonnes of CO2 are generated each year by APIs.

    But what kind of APIs? In 2020 Smartbear's State Of API Report estimated 82% of APIs were REST APIs. A closer look suggests that 93-95% of APIs in their survey are essentially synchronous command response APIs, and only 5-7% are asynchronous, event driven APIs. Again, the precise numbers will differ, but the overall distribution is clear. A very significant proportion of those APIs are queried or query via polling, where repeated calls are made to see if there has been a state change. According to a 2013 estimate by Zapier, 98.5% of those requests return empty, meaning all the emissions involved across the request lifecycle are entirely wasted. The situation has almost certainly improved dramatically in the intervening 9 years, but is an example of the type of inefficiencies involved.

    Which gets us closer to AsyncApi!

    Increasingly, there is a shift from command driven, to event driven architectures, where instead of waiting for a specific command to trigger a behaviour, that behaviour is tied and automated asynchronously to changes in conditions, that is to say, events. This has huge potential and possibilities, which is why it is growing so rapidly as an architectural paradigm. Gartner projected that by the end of this year (2022)

    • Event notifications will form part of over 60% of new digital business solutions.
    • Over 50% of business organizations will participate in event-driven digital business ecosystems.
    • 50% of organizations managing APIs will incorporate mediation of event notifications into their operations.
    • Most leading providers of application platforms will include high-productivity tools for event-driven design.

    The environmental potential of switching from traditional REST APIs and similar command-request approaches, is that you can tie behaviour and consumption to climate aware triggers. You could apply graceful degradation when the grid is not running on renewables, and full performance when it is. You could have an IoT device automatically switch to low energy mode when it doesn't require constant activation. Instead of relying on people to remember to turn off lights or devices, they could be switched on and off upon detecting the user's presence or absence. Indeed, such architectures underpin most smart devices.

    One of the greatest barriers to adoption of this paradigm, which could significantly cut into the amount of data transferred and energy consumed by the internet, is the historic absence of a common language to facilitate interoperability. This is where AsyncAPI (finally!) comes in. It provides a standard and a language to describe event based, asynchronous communication, greatly enabling not just inter-api communication, but also drastically cutting developer design and implementation time, by making possible a wide range of automations, akin to the more familiar OpenApi. Such a common language acts as a universal translator, which allows the emergence of a network of devices, workflows and tools attuned to one another in an emergent, inductive way.

    In addition, the AsyncApi community has the historic opportunity, at this early stage in the spec's evolution, to dedicate serious attention to any way in which it could not just describe, but nudge the nature of event driven communication that is coming, or like Gibson said, is here, just not evenly distributed. It may be that there are elements that could be added to the specification that could greatly increase the environmental potential of all applications built in line with its specification. For instance, being able to flag whether a network is delay tolerant or not, could make a huge difference toward allowing a wide range of energy aware applications, as well as widening digital access and inclusion, and facilitating innovation, specially in the Global South.

    This is a very early, yet therefore also a key moment. Event driven, asynchronous communication is, with cloud and serverless, part of the next (current?) wave of technological paradigms. If AsyncApi can enable and influence the transition from a command-request, synchronous, and climate blind ecosystem, to an event-driven, asynchronous, and environmentally conscious paradigm, then, after all this, yes, I do believe it could be part of making an observable dent on world emissions, and therefore on climate change.

Items.Title: Kotlin Null かも知れない値に当てる演算子 | Kotlin Null かも知れない値に当てる演算子
Items.Description:
Kotlin には様々な null の扱いの演算子があるのでまとめてみた

ここで実行して试しました

通常の実行

val maybeNullValue = null println ( maybeNullValue ) // null

普通に print すると null が返ってくる

で null が返ってくるくらいなら ぬるぽ のエラーを返す

が入がるるっ使ぶいるっ傋るるエエラががなるお算子が、null

nullableValue?:"" で null だった场合は空の文字列を返す

val maybeNullValue = null println ( maybeNullValue ?: "empty" ) // empty

多くの场合は空の文字列を入れるだろう。
わかりやすいようにサンプルでは"空"の文字列を入れている

こうすれば安全だし书きやすい。

nullableValue?.let { } の中に处理を书いて null な时は実行されないようにする

こうすれば null がないときにはスキップされる

val maybeNullValue = "text" maybeNullValue ?. let { println ( maybeNullValue ) } println ( "executed" ) //text //executed

text を入れてみると print されるが

val maybeNullValue = null maybeNullValue ?. let { println ( maybeNullValue ) } println ( "executed" ) // //executed

ワンライナーで书きやすい。

丽莎费雷拉 - 一旦暂停，kaede_io 将无法评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

一旦解除暂停，kaede_io 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，kaede_io 的所有帖子都将被隐藏，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 kaede_io 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

感谢您保护 DEV 社区的安全。 以下是标记 kaede_io 的方法：

举报其他不当行为

取消标记 kaede_io 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Kotlin には様々な null の扱いの演算子があるのでまとめてみた

https://play.kotlinlang.org/

ここで実行して試しました

通常の実行 

 val maybeNullValue = null println(maybeNullValue)
// null

普通に print すると null が返ってくる

NotBeNullValue!! で null が返ってくるくらいなら ぬるぽ のエラーを返す

https://engawapg.net/kotlin/116/what-is-kotlin-exclamation-mark/

 val maybeNullValue = null println(maybeNullValue!!)
// Overload resolution ambiguity
// 多分ぬるぽ

null が入っている値に使うとぶっ壊れる演算子
エラーになるけど、null が渡るよりマシな場面に使えると推測。

nullableValue?:"" で null だった場合は空の文字列を返す 

 val maybeNullValue = null println(maybeNullValue?:"empty")
// empty

?: を使えば、それが null だった時に次の値を入れられる。

多くの場合は空の文字列を入れるだろう。
わかりやすいようにサンプルでは"empty"の文字列を入れている

こうすれば安全だし書きやすい。

nullableValue?.let { } の中に処理を書いて null な時は実行されないようにする

https://www.tutorialspoint.com/best-way-to-null-check-in-kotlin#:~:text=let%22%20operator,a%20non%2DNULL%20able%20value.

nullableValue?.let { // execute(nullableValue) }

こうすれば null がないときにはスキップされる

 val maybeNullValue = "text" maybeNullValue ?.let { println(maybeNullValue) } println("executed") //text
//executed

text を入れてみると print されるが

 val maybeNullValue = null maybeNullValue ?.let { println(maybeNullValue) } println("executed")
//
//executed

null が入っているとスキップされる。
ワンライナーで書きやすい。
